UK bond markets jolted at the news, with gilt yields nearing the levels of last yearтАЩs тАЬminiтАЭ Budget crisis. Inflation in the UK is now about double the equivalent US rate, and well above almost all of western Europe. The return of high inflation and slow growth is reminiscent of the 1970s. The FTтАЩs economics editor Chris Giles is therefore asking: Is the UK once again the тАШsick manтАЩ of Europe?Traders predict more trouble is ahead for UK bonds, after stronger than expected inflation data yesterday ┬й Reuters My choices this week тАМ тАМ US legislators have been drawing up rules that affect their personal investments because of antiquated loopholes in conflict of interest laws. This excellent FT analysis exposes how lawmakers could be benefiting from Capitol HillтАЩs failure to keep pace with a transformed investment sector.Russia is fortifying its frontline in anticipation of the long-awaited Ukrainian counter-offensive. By analysing satellite imagery and mapping changes on the ground, our correspondent in Kyiv explores how the attack might play out. (free to read)тАЬForever chemicalsтАЭ that are vital to the production of microchips could have life-threatening health and environmental effects, growing evidence suggests. Facing calls for more stringent regulation, how is a rapidly growing industry responding to the threat?(If youтАЩd like to read this article for free, please download our iPhone app, FT Edit, where it is in todayтАЩs edition. You donтАЩt need to register or give payment details, you can simply download and start reading FT Edit, and the first month is free.)The G7 heads of government painted a picture of unipolar economic dominance at their summit in Hiroshima last weekend. But despite its best efforts, the G7 cannot run the world, writes the FTтАЩs chief economics commentator Martin Wolf.ChinaтАЩs rapidly ageing population тАФ in part a legacy of its one-child policy тАФ is forcing the government to make painful reforms tackling pensions, healthcare and the workforce. This poignant report from Rudong, ChinaтАЩs oldest county, offers a snapshot of ChinaтАЩs demographic decline. (free to read)Last weekend I was in Washington DC for a brilliant day at the FT Weekend festival. Among other highlights, the 2016 Democratic presidential candidate Hillary Clinton discussed China, Putin, Trump and Netflix series The Diplomat, with the FTтАЩs US national editor Ed Luce. Watch the interview on ft.com here.Thanks for readingRoula PS DonтАЩt forget our special subscription offer, which means you get a third off a Standard digital subscription to the Financial Times. Subject: [opensuse] GoBook II touchscreen driver
Body: Itronix model IX260 hardened laptop... In searching for a driver & information for the Semtech touch screen, I find two possible drivers via google, no information on X-org. One driver, semtech-1.0 from the MIT/X conxortium, dated last in 2005, and Evtouch with a last-update of September, 2007. Is anyone here using either of those drivers? Or any knowledge that it might already be built into the current versions of X??? (The screen responds, but is way off target and crashes X in the process.) TIA, Tom in NM
text:
[opensuse] GoBook II touchscreen driver

Itronix model IX260 hardened laptop... In searching for a driver & information for the Semtech touch screen, I find two possible drivers via google, no information on X-org. One driver, semtech-1.0 from the MIT/X conxortium, dated last in 2005, and Evtouch with a last-update of September, 2007. Is anyone here using either of those drivers? Or any knowledge that it might already be built into the current versions of X??? (The screen responds, but is way off target and crashes X in the process.) TIA, Tom in NM

Subject: Re: [opensuse] Unstable system - who is the culprit?
Body: On Thu, 2008-02-07 at 10:13 +0100, Per Jessen wrote: > Randall R Schulz wrote: > > > Offhand, I don't know how the efficiency of today's power supplies > > varies with the percentage of their rated output they're producing. In > > other words, does a higher-capacity PSU consume more mains power for a > > given level of consumption on its output side? If so, you wouldn't > > want to have such a large PSU, since that would be wasteful. > > I haven't looked at switchmode technology for quite some time, but I > would expect a fairly fixed efficiency in the 80-90% range, quite > probably more. There will be a minimum consumption required in order > to achieve optimal operation, but otherwise I think the output power > will be e.g. 80% of the input power, and the rest is turned into heat. or to say it another way...(at 80% efficiency) a 150 watt supply or a 500 watt supply should either one pull the same current from the mains, if they alternately supply the same 80 watt load (ie MB)...approximately 100 watts from the mains in each example. It would NOT be wasteful to the environment...(but maybe to your wallet). Tom in NM
text:
Re: [opensuse] Unstable system - who is the culprit?

On Thu, 2008-02-07 at 10:13 +0100, Per Jessen wrote: > Randall R Schulz wrote: > > > Offhand, I don't know how the efficiency of today's power supplies > > varies with the percentage of their rated output they're producing. In > > other words, does a higher-capacity PSU consume more mains power for a > > given level of consumption on its output side? If so, you wouldn't > > want to have such a large PSU, since that would be wasteful. > > I haven't looked at switchmode technology for quite some time, but I > would expect a fairly fixed efficiency in the 80-90% range, quite > probably more. There will be a minimum consumption required in order > to achieve optimal operation, but otherwise I think the output power > will be e.g. 80% of the input power, and the rest is turned into heat. or to say it another way...(at 80% efficiency) a 150 watt supply or a 500 watt supply should either one pull the same current from the mains, if they alternately supply the same 80 watt load (ie MB)...approximately 100 watts from the mains in each example. It would NOT be wasteful to the environment...(but maybe to your wallet). Tom in NM

Subject: [opensuse] Re: multipurpose fax/printer/copier
Body: Doug McGarrett wrote: > Does anyone have a suggestion for the above referenced machine, that > installs without any hassle, special drivers, or anything else, to SuSE 9.3? > It should be low cost, hopefully. I don't want to look for repositories, > special RPMs, I want the system to recognize and install it, period. I have > had it with things that are supposed to work, but don't. > > Thanx--doug > > Blessed are the peacemakers ... for they shall be shot at from both sides. > --A.M. Greeley HP is the way to go for this. Their Linux support is outstanding and the functionality is very close to "that other OS". You simply do not have to worry, whether it will work. But: Be sure to have a detailed look at the "supported models" list, BEFORE you buy, though. http://hplip.sourceforge.net/supported_devices/index.html Furthermore, as you are still on SuSE 9.3, most probably, you will have to do a manual installation. http://hplip.sourceforge.net/install/index.html Imho, this is as close as you will get in regard to... ."I don't want to look for repositories, special RPMs, I want the system to recognize and install it, period." Kind regards Eberhard
